sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content:
Ultraviolet (UV) Radiation Exposure
Immunosuppression
Merkel Cell Polyomavirus (MCPyV) Infection
Advanced Age
Fair Skin and Light Hair Color
Genetic Predisposition

cards.detail: Sun exposure is considered a major risk factor. This is supported by the increased incidence in geographic areas with higher UV indices, the tendency for MCC to arise on sun-exposed skin, and the frequent co-occurrence with other UV-induced skin lesions. In virus-negative MCC, tumors exhibit a UV-specific mutational signature (C to T transitions).

MCC is disproportionately more common in immunocompromised individuals. This includes solid organ transplant recipients, patients with hematologic malignancies (especially chronic lymphocytic leukemia [CLL]), and individuals with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection. Immunosuppression is a risk factor for poor outcomes and increased recurrence.

Clonal integration of MCPyV into tumor cell DNA is found in 43% to 100% of MCC cases. The virus is considered a cofactor in the pathogenesis of a subset of MCC tumors, though its precise role in causation vs. passenger is under study.
The incidence of MCC increases significantly with age, with the vast majority of cases diagnosed in individuals over 50 years old.

cards.factor: Fair Skin and Light Hair Color
cards.detail: Individuals with lighter skin phototypes (Fitzpatrick types I and II) are at substantially higher risk, consistent with increased susceptibility to UV radiation damage.

Mutations in known cancer-associated genes that may be heritable have been reported in approximately 20% of patients with MCC under 50 years of age, suggesting a potential genetic predisposition in a subset of early-onset cases.
